perturbation.original_text: 6. Any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance, or breach of this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by binding arbitration in Las Vegas, Nevada, in accordance with the rules then in effect of the American Arbitration Association, The arbitrator(s) may grant injunctions or other relief in such dispute or controversy. The decision of the arbitrator(s) shall be final, conclusive, and binding on the parties to the arbitration. Judgment may be entered on the arbitrator's decision in any court having jurisdiction. The parties to the arbitration shall each pay an equal share of the costs and expenses of such <PAGE> arbitration, and each of them shall separately pay their counsel fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance, or breach of this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by binding arbitration in Las Vegas, Nevada. The decision of the arbitrator(s) shall be final and binding, and no appeal may be taken from the arbitrator’s decision. Judgment may be entered on the arbitrator's decision in any court having jurisdiction. The parties to the arbitration shall each pay their own counsel fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to the American Arbitration Association and stating that there shall be no appeal taken from the arbitrator's decision attempts to restrict the right to appeal an arbitration decision, which is regulated by law.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 9 U.S. Code § 10, which outlines specific grounds for vacating an arbitration award. By stating that 'no appeal may be taken,' the contract attempts to eliminate rights to challenge the arbitration outcome as provided by federal law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text attempts to eliminate the right to appeal an arbitration decision, which contradicts 9 U.S. Code § 10. Both snippets of 9 U.S. Code § 10 describe grounds for vacating an arbitration award. The changed text states that 'no appeal may be taken,' which directly conflicts with the legal rights to challenge arbitration outcomes as provided by federal law. This is a clear attempt to override established legal procedures for arbitration disputes.

perturbation.original_text: 1. None of the parties shall divulge to any person, (other than those whose provenance it is to know it, or with proper authority) or use any trade secrets or confidential information or any financial or trade information relating to the other, which they acquire as a result of the discussing of or the entering into agreements with each other. Each Party shall endeavor to prevent its officers, employees, agents, representatives, and associates from doing anything, which, if done by Party, would be a breach of this agreement. This restriction shall continue to apply after the expiration of this Agreement, and other Agreements entered into between the Parties, without limit in point of time, but shall cease to apply to secrets or information, which came into the public domain through no fault of the Party concerned.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. None of the parties shall divulge to any person, (other than those whose provenance it is to know it, or with proper authority) or use any trade secrets or confidential information or any financial or trade information relating to the other, which they acquire as a result of the discussing of or the entering into agreements with each other. Each Party shall endeavor to prevent its officers, employees, agents, representatives, and associates from doing anything, which, if done by Party, would be a breach of this agreement. This restriction shall continue to apply after the expiration of this Agreement, and other Agreements entered into between the Parties, without limit in point of time, and shall not cease to apply to secrets or information, which came into the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'through no fault of the Party concerned' makes the confidentiality clause excessively broad. Even if a party discloses information, unintentionally making it public, the clause would still prohibit any use, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the widely-adopted Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), specifically the definition of 'trade secret.' Section 1(4) typically requires that the information not be generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. By not ceasing to apply to secrets that came into the public domain, the contract tries to protect information that no longer qualifies as a trade secret under the UTSA, thereby creating an overly broad restriction not recognized by law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the confidentiality clause beyond what is legally protected as a trade secret. The Law Explanation states that the modified text attempts to protect information that no longer qualifies as a trade secret under the UTSA, because it does not cease to apply to secrets that came into the public domain, thus creating an overly broad restriction not recognized by law. The original text provided an exception for information that came into the public domain through no fault of the party concerned, thus aligning with the legal requirements for trade secret protection. Since snippet 1 and 2 are missing/invalid, the determination relies on the other inputs provided, namely the Law Explanation, Perturbation Explanation, and the comparison between Original and Changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The Parties each undertake to the other that for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, they will not, without prior written consent of the other, directly or indirectly through third parties, make or seek to make contact or communication with those Banks, Financial Institutions, government representatives, clients, investors, traders, associates, legal advisors and financial advisors with whom they have been placed into contact with by the other or to whom the names, addresses and other pertinent information has been released to them by the other.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Parties each undertake to the other that for a period of ten (10) years from the date of this Agreement, they will not, without prior written consent of the other, directly or indirectly through third parties, make or seek to make contact or communication with those Banks, Financial Institutions, government representatives, clients, investors, traders, associates, legal advisors and financial advisors with whom they have been placed into contact with by the other or to whom the names, addresses and other pertinent information has been released to them by the other.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period to ten years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification potentially contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which generally prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are exceptions, a ten-year restriction on contacting clients could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly in certain industries.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period from five to ten years. The Law Explanation states that this modification potentially contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in 16600(a) that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Perturbation Explanation also indicates the change might be deemed unreasonable under California Law. This extension to ten years creates a significant restraint, making it likely unenforceable under CA law and therefore creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance, or breach of this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by binding arbitration in Las Vegas, Nevada, in accordance with the rules then in effect of the American Arbitration Association, The arbitrator(s) may grant injunctions or other relief in such dispute or controversy. The decision of the arbitrator(s) shall be final, conclusive, and binding on the parties to the arbitration. Judgment may be entered on the arbitrator's decision in any court having jurisdiction. The parties to the arbitration shall each pay an equal share of the costs and expenses of such <PAGE> arbitration, and each of them shall separately pay their counsel fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance, or breach of this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by arbitration in accordance with the rules then in effect of the American Arbitration Association in a location solely determined by Gateway Venture Holdings, Inc. The arbitrator(s) may grant injunctions or other relief in such dispute or controversy. The decision of the arbitrator(s) shall be final, conclusive, and binding on the parties to the arbitration. Judgment may be entered on the arbitrator's decision in any court having jurisdiction. The parties to the arbitration shall each pay an equal share of the costs and expenses of such arbitration, and each of them shall separately pay their counsel fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing one party (Gateway Venture Holdings, Inc.) to unilaterally determine the arbitration location could be considered unconscionable and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts Nevada Revised Statutes 38.218. While this statute doesn't explicitly prohibit one party from choosing the location, it does require that parties enter into arbitration agreements voluntarily and without coercion. A provision allowing one party to unilaterally select the location of arbitration, particularly if that location is inconvenient or expensive for the other party, could be deemed to be unconscionable or to evidence a lack of voluntary agreement, thus rendering the arbitration agreement unenforceable under Nevada law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the principle of voluntary agreement in arbitration, potentially violating Nevada law. While snippet 1, NRS: CHAPTER 38, doesn't explicitly forbid one-sided location selection, the Law Explanation highlights that such a provision could be deemed unconscionable, indicating a lack of voluntary agreement. The original contract stipulated arbitration in Las Vegas, Nevada, a seemingly neutral location. The changed text empowers Gateway Venture Holdings, Inc. to unilaterally choose the location, potentially creating an unfair advantage and undermining the voluntary nature of the agreement.
